_meta.title: Build & CI/CD — Pipelines That Don't Suck

questions.question: A CI pipeline takes 25 minutes to run. Developers start batching commits and pushing once a day instead of continuously. Which CI principle is being violated?
options: Determinism — the pipeline should produce the same result every time
Speed — pipelines should complete in under 10 minutes to enable frequent integration
Isolation — each pipeline run should be in a fresh environment
Parallelism — stages should run concurrently, not sequentially
questions.explanation: The pipeline is too slow (25 minutes vs. the < 10 minute guideline), which discourages frequent pushing. When CI is slow, developers batch changes, defeating the purpose of continuous integration. The other principles are important but not the root cause here.

questions.question: A test passes 9 out of 10 times on the same commit with no code changes. The team adds a retry step to the CI pipeline so it passes on the second attempt. What anti-pattern does this represent?
options: Build-only CI — testing without deploying
Snowflake environment — inconsistent build environments
Ignoring flakes — masking non-deterministic test failures instead of fixing them
Manual gate — requiring human approval in the pipeline
questions.explanation: This is the 'Ignoring flakes' anti-pattern. A test that intermittently fails reveals a real bug (e.g., race condition, timing dependency). Retrying hides the symptom but doesn't fix the cause. The correct approach is to quarantine the flaky test, track it, and fix the root cause.

questions.question: Your team deploys to production by SSH-ing into a server and copying files. The deployment works, but rollbacks require figuring out which files to replace. Which deployment strategy would most improve this situation?
options: Rolling deployment — replace instances one at a time
Blue-green deployment — maintain two identical environments and switch traffic between them
Feature flags — toggle new behaviour on and off without redeployment
Canary deployment — route a small percentage of traffic to the new version
questions.explanation: Blue-green deployment solves both problems: deploying means pushing to the inactive environment (green), and rolling back means switching the router back to the active one (blue) — instant, no file hunting. Rolling deployment doesn't simplify rollback. Feature flags help decouple release from deployment but don't fix the manual SSH process. Canary limits blast radius but doesn't address the rollback problem.

questions.question: Which metric best answers the question: 'When something breaks in production, how quickly can we recover?'
options: Deployment frequency — how often we ship to production
Build success rate — what percentage of CI runs pass
Mean Time To Recovery (MTTR) — average time from failure to restored service
Test coverage — percentage of code covered by automated tests
questions.explanation: MTTR directly measures recovery speed from production incidents. Deployment frequency measures throughput, build success rate measures CI health, and test coverage measures code quality — none of which directly answer how fast you recover from a production outage.

questions.question: A company has CI that builds and tests every commit, but deployment is still a manual process requiring an ops engineer to run scripts on production servers. According to the lesson, which anti-pattern best describes this situation?
options: Snowflake environment — the production server has a unique, untracked configuration
Build-only CI — the pipeline verifies the code compiles and tests pass but never actually deploys
Manual gate — a human must approve or execute deployment steps that could be automated
Trunk-based development — developers aren't integrating frequently enough
questions.explanation: This is build-only CI: the pipeline proves the code compiles and tests pass, but never verifies that it actually runs in a deployment environment. The problem isn't manual gates (which are about approval) or snowflake environments (which are about configuration drift). It's that the pipeline stops at build/test and never reaches deploy, leaving the delivery gap.

questions.question: You're designing a pipeline and need to put stages in the optimal order. Which ordering follows the fail-fast principle?
options: Integration tests → Unit tests → Lint → Build → Deploy
Lint → Unit tests → Integration tests → Build → Deploy
Build → Lint → Unit tests → Integration tests → Deploy
Deploy → Integration tests → Unit tests → Lint → Build
questions.explanation: Fail-fast means putting the cheapest, fastest checks first so failures are caught quickly without wasting compute. Lint (~30s) is faster than unit tests (~2min), which are faster than integration tests (~5min). Build precedes deploy. Option 1 puts expensive integration tests first. Option 3 has build before lint, wasting build time on code that doesn't even pass lint. Option 4 is nonsensical (deploy first).
